nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Bosco
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Angelo, Salvatore, Carmine, Sal, Ignazio, Antonio, Attilio, Cosimo, Francesco, Gaspare, Luigi, Saverio.

Italian: topographic name for someone living or working in a wood, from bosco ‘wood’, from Late Latin boscus ‘shrub, undergrowth’ (of Gallic or ancient Germanic origin), or a habitational name from a place called with this word. In some cases it may have been an occupational name for a woodsman or forester and, by extension, a nickname for a surly or rough person. Compare Lo Bosco .

variants.name: Boschetti
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Mario, Angelina, Antonio, Guido, Rosario.
Italian: patronymic or plural form of a diminutive of   Bosco  .

variants.name: Lo Bosco
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Angelo, Carmine, Gennaro, Sal, Salvatore, Antonino, Nichola, Pasquale, Piera, Raffaela, Rocco.
Italian (southern; also Lobosco): variant of Bosco , with the addition of the definite article lo.

nameMeanings.name: Wood
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: mainly a topographic name for someone who lived in or by a wood, from Middle English wode ‘wood’ (Old English wudu). In North America, the English form of the surname has absorbed cognates from other languages, such as French Bois and Polish Les .
English: in a few cases, a nickname for an eccentric or perhaps a violent person, from Middle English wode ‘frenzied, wild’ (Old English wōd).
Americanized form of French   Gadbois  .

variants.name: Woode
meanings: English: variant of   Wood  . — Note: The population figure published by the US Census Bureau for this surname in the year 2000 is as high as 312, while the figure for 2010 is supposedly lower than 100.

variants.name: Bois
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Alcide, Antoine, Donat, Marcel, Ovide, Ovila, Raoul, Serge.

French: topographic name for someone living or working in a wood, from (Old) French bois ‘wood’ (from Late Latin boscum ‘bush, shrub, undergrowth’, of ancient Germanic origin), or a habitational name from (Le) Bois, the name of several places in various parts of France. This surname is also found in Haiti. Compare Debois and the most frequent French form Dubois . Compare also Boie , Boies , and Boyce .
English: variant of   Boyce   and   Boyes  .

variants.name: Woods
meanings: English: variant of Wood with plural or post-medieval excrescent -s.

Irish: adopted as a translation of Ó Cuill ‘descendant of Coll’ (see Quill ), or in Ulster of Mac Con Coille ‘son of Cú Choille’, a personal name meaning ‘hound of the wood’, which has also been mistranslated Cox , as if formed with coileach ‘cock, rooster’.

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert

en.lifeSummary: Richard de Bosco  Wood was born in 1260, in Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom. He had at least 1 son with Bosco. He died after 1316.

fr.lifeSummary: Richard de Bosco  Wood est né en 1260, à Everwyck, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Il a eu au moins 1 fils avec Bosco. Il est décédé après 1316.

de.lifeSummary: Richard de Bosco  Wood wurde 1260 in Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom geboren. Er hatte mit Bosco mindestens 1 Sohn. Er starb nach 1316.

it.lifeSummary: Richard de Bosco  Wood è nato il nel 1260 nel Yorkshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito. Lui avuto almeno 1 figlio con Bosco. Morì dopo il 1316.
